name: Brianna Romrell
ai_summary: Brianna Romrell brings six years of childcare experience including two years working with children with autism and disabilities across classroom and after-school settings. She seeks a full-time position in Salt Lake City, UT and excels at conflict resolution by empowering children to talk through issues and develop their own solutions.

transcripts.q3:
Hey there, my name is Bree. I overall have six years experience, working in childcare, two of those years were, specifically with children with autism and similar disabilities. I have worked in classroom settings, after-school programs, summer programs. I have worked with the Salt Lake County Christmas Box House as well as Salt Lake County. Juvenile, receiving Center, I have worked
 With pretty much all ages infants through 12, although most of my experience does come from school age, so K through five. But I do want to broaden my experience with the younger kids to I like working with kids because I feel like they have a lot to offer. They have a lot to teach us as adults. I feel
 They have just as much to teach us as we have to help them. Learn one of my favorite things about working with kids and helping them get to that aha moment where they finally understand something, whether it's as simple as tying your shoe or something as hard as an algebra problem, that they've been stuck on forever, I really help. I really like being able to help them get to that moment and help them learn.
 Through.
 The different different ways that they can do things.
transcripts.q4: So I live in Salt Lake City Utah. I live kind of in the middle of Salt Lake and I am willing and able to travel all throughout Salt Lake.
transcripts.q5: I am looking for full-time right now. I do have pretty open, availability Monday through Thursday. I'm available 8:30 a.m. until about 9:00 p.m. and then Friday, I'm available 9 a.m. until 8 p.m.

And then I go over and I talked to the other kid and I say, hey, what happened? I want to know your side of the story. I get both sides, I left.
 I let both parties calm down and then I have them, I pulled him aside and I have them explain to each other their own side of the story.
 And I have them talk about it, and they, I generally try to have them come up with their own solution. Like, how can you fix this on your own? I'm here to support you but I don't want to fix your problems for you to an extent, obviously. Sometimes that's not possible, and sometimes it just has to
 Sometimes we as adults. Do you have to step in and in a sense, solve that problem, but 90% of my ninety percent of the time, in my experience, the kids have been able to talk it out, work it out, figure out what they need to do in the future to not have this happen again.

 Knowledge about crisis management. When it comes to children, and I definitely think that that helps me when I'm working with them. And I like to think that I also,
